task: I’m trying to decide whether to take on a small property in the Fort Bragg area, but I don’t want to miss any local friction points that can change the math. What should I check first so I’m not blindsided by permit timing, inspection issues, or market quirks?

turns.agent_response:
Start with the things that can change your timeline and your leverage, not the pretty numbers.

### 1) Permits and inspection path
Check:
- what work was done with permits, and what wasn’t
- whether final inspections are closed
- whether any open permits, code violations, or unpermitted repairs exist
- how long local permit approval and re-inspection actually take

**Why it matters:** if the file is messy, you may not be buying a property — you’re buying a delay.

### 2) Local utility and service constraints
Verify:
- water source and sewer/septic status
- power service capacity
- internet/cell reliability if tenant demand depends on it
- any utility turn-on or transfer delays

**Why it matters:** in smaller markets, utility friction can slow occupancy or trigger extra costs fast.

### 3) Tenant and occupancy status
Confirm:
- who is actually living there
- lease terms and expiration
- deposits held and by whom
- payment history and delinquency
- any notice issues or local tenant protections

**Why it matters:** a clean-looking rent roll can hide a control problem.

### 4) Market quirks specific to the area
Look at:
- seasonal demand swings
- local employer dependence
- military-related rental demand if applicable
- resale liquidity for your price band
- insurance and maintenance costs tied to the area

**Why it matters:** a “good” rent number can still be weak if vacancy or turnover is irregular.

### 5) Property-condition red flags
Prioritize:
- roof, drainage, moisture, foundation
- deferred maintenance
- signs of prior water intrusion
- electrical, plumbing, and septic issues
- any work that could trigger permit or inspection problems later

**Why it matters:** these are the things that turn a manageable deal into a control problem.

### Fastest way to check first
I’d do it in this order:
1. **Title + permit file**
2. **Occupancy/lease review**
3. **Inspection walk with a contractor**
4. **Utility and service verification**
5. **Local rent and vacancy check**

### Simple rule
If the property has:
- open permits,
- unclear occupancy,
- septic or water uncertainty,
- or weak resale liquidity,

then the discount has to be big enough to cover **delay, extra cash, and loss of control** — not just repair cost.

If you want, I can turn this into a tight due-diligence grid with columns for **item / owner / proof / hold point / impact on price**.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly right. But I’d tighten the order more than that.

First thing: **planning + permit history + use legality**. Not just open permits — zoning, legal use, any prior conversion, ADU, garage, deck, stair, egress stuff. Fort Bragg / Mendocino County can get stupid fast if the file is messy.

Then **water and septic** if it’s not clearly on city services. Those two can blow up the math harder than cosmetics. I’d want proof, not seller assurances.

Then **insurance availability and cost**. In this area that can matter as much as taxes. If the carrier gets picky, your cap rate is fiction.

Then **actual rentability**: who the tenant pool is, seasonal demand, and how fast units really turn. Not some generic “coastal market” story.

Then **condition items that trigger work** — roof, drainage, moisture, foundation, electrical, stairs, decks, any unpermitted additions.

I’d want a cleaner due-diligence grid than the general list you gave. Add columns for:
- **proof needed**
- **who verifies it**
- **days to clear**
- **cash at risk**
- **deal-killer or not**

That’s the part people skip and then act surprised when the county or insurer drags the thing out for weeks.

If you want, build me that grid for a small Fort Bragg acquisition — make it blunt, not consultant fluff.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a blunt DD grid for a small Fort Bragg / Mendocino County acquisition.

## Fort Bragg DD Grid

| Item | Proof needed | Who verifies it | Days to clear | Cash at risk | Deal-killer? |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| **Planning / zoning / legal use** | Zoning map, parcel lookup, planning dept confirmation, prior permits, legal nonconforming status if applicable | Land use attorney or planning consultant; you can preliminarily verify with county/city planning | 3–14+ | High | **Yes** if use is not legal or conversion is unresolved |
| **Permit history** | Full permit history, finals, open/expired permits, code enforcement records | County/city building dept | 3–10 | High | **Yes** if there are unresolved open permits on core systems |
| **Prior conversion / ADU / garage / deck / stair / egress legality** | Approved plans, finals, as-built docs, photos, assessor records | Building dept + inspector | 3–14 | High | **Yes** if it’s relied on for rentability and can’t be legalized cleanly |
| **Water source** | City bill or well report, well log, pump test, water quality test, service confirmation | Utility provider / well contractor | 2–10 | High | **Yes** if water is uncertain or fails test |
| **Septic / sewer** | Septic permit, tank/pump records, inspection, perc/soil docs, sewer connection confirmation | Septic inspector / county environmental health | 3–14 | High | **Yes** if septic is failing, undersized, or undocumented |
| **Insurance availability / cost** | Carrier quotes, exclusions, wildfire/wind/flood terms, deductible, replacement cost assumptions | Insurance broker | 2–7 | High | **Yes** if coverage is unavailable or priced outside underwriting |
| **Tenant pool / rentability** | Actual comps, absorption, vacancy, seasonal demand, employer base, lease-up history | Broker + PM + your own comp check | 2–7 | Medium | No, but price should move hard if demand is thin |
| **Turn speed / occupancy friction** | Days-on-market, average vacancy, tenant turnover, move-in requirements, utility transfer timing | PM / local agents | 2–5 | Medium | No, unless the unit is hard to fill at your rent |
| **Roof** | Roof age, contractor assessment, photos, leak history, permit if replaced | Roofer or GC | 1–3 | High | **Yes** if near-term replacement is likely |
| **Drainage / moisture / rot** | Crawlspace/photos, moisture readings, grading, gutter/downspout condition | Inspector + GC | 1–3 | High | **Yes** if active intrusion or structural rot is present |
| **Foundation** | Visible movement, cracks, pier/grade beam report, prior repair docs | Structural engineer if suspicious | 1–5 | High | **Yes** if movement is active or repair scope is large |
| **Electrical** | Panel size, service condition, knob-and-tube/unsafe wiring, permit history | Licensed electrician | 1–3 | Medium/High | **Yes** if unsafe or uninsurable |
| **Stairs / decks / egress** | Photos, code compliance, railings, landing dimensions, permits | Inspector / GC | 1–3 | Medium | **Yes** if it affects legal occupancy or safety |
| **Deferred maintenance / capex backlog** | Contractor walk, line-item estimate, photo log | GC + you | 1–5 | Medium | No, but it must hit price |
| **Title / access / easements** | Preliminary title report, easements, access road rights, shared driveway docs | Title officer / attorney | 2–7 | High | **Yes** if access is impaired or unclear |
| **Flood / wildfire exposure** | FEMA map, fire zone, defensible space issues, prior claims | Insurance broker + map review | 1–3 | High | **Yes** if coverage or mitigation becomes uneconomic |

## What I’d check first, in order

### 1) Planning + permit history + legal use
This is the first gate because if the use is wrong, everything else is noise.

Ask for:
- parcel number
- zoning designation
- full permit history
- finals status
- any code enforcement history
- records for additions, garage conversions, ADUs, decks, stairs, egress changes

**Red flag:** seller says “it’s always been that way” or “the county never cared.”

If the building depends on unpermitted space for rent, that is not a cosmetic issue — that is a valuation issue.

### 2) Water and septic
If it’s not clearly on reliable city services, treat this as a hard stop until proven otherwise.

Ask for:
- utility confirmation or well records
- septic permit and inspection
- tank size and drainfield info
- any recent pumping/repair records
- water quality test if well-served

**Red flag:** “We’ve never had a problem” with no paperwork.

### 3) Insurance
In this area, insurance can kill the deal even when the building looks fine.

Get:
- real quotes, not estimates
- deductible terms
- exclusions
- replacement cost basis
- any wildfire or coastal surcharge assumptions

**Red flag:** broker says “probably insurable” but can’t bind it cleanly.

### 4) Rentability and tenant pool
Only after the above do I care about the rent story.

Check:
- who actually rents there
- seasonality
- local vacancy
- days on market
- whether the unit type is liquid at your price point

**Red flag:** the rent only works in a perfect occupancy scenario.

### 5) Condition items that trigger work
Walk the stuff that turns into real money:
- roof
- drainage
- moisture
- foundation
- electrical
- stairs/decks
- prior unpermitted work

If one of these is already failing, assume the bill gets worse, not better.

---

## Who to talk to first

1. **Broker/seller** — for documents and history  
2. **Planning / building dept** — for use, permits, finals, code issues  
3. **Insurance broker** — for real insurability and pricing  
4. **PM or local agent** — for rentability and tenant demand  
5. **Inspector / GC / specialist** — for physical condition and repair scope  

---

## Fast walk-away triggers

Walk if you find any of these and can’t cure them cheaply:
- illegal use that supports the income
- unresolved permit problems on core improvements
- septic or water uncertainty
- insurance that is unavailable or way above underwriting
- access/title problems
- active structural or moisture damage that’s not a small fix

---

## Practical rule
If the issue affects **legal use, insurability, or utility service**, it moves the price.  
If it affects only **appearance or convenience**, it’s usually a repair item.  
If it affects **all three**, it’s probably a bad deal unless the discount is ugly.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page fill-in template** you can use on a specific address, with checkboxes and a simple pass / renegotiate / walk decision.

user_simulator.text:
That’s the grid I wanted. Tight enough to use, not dressed up.

The order is right: planning/use legality first, then water/septic, then insurance, then rentability, then condition. That’s how I’d run it too. The columns are the useful part — proof, verifier, days to clear, cash at risk, deal-killer. That’s the actual decision frame.

A couple things I’d make even sharper:
- Under **planning / legal use**, I’d separate **current legal use** from **grandfathered/nonconforming status**. Those are not the same.
- Under **permit history**, I’d want a line for **whether the final inspection was tied to the exact work that was done**, not just whether a permit exists.
- Under **insurance**, I’d add **carrier appetite for the exact construction type** if it’s older coastal stuff. Sometimes the building is fine and the carrier still hates it.
- Under **water/septic**, if it’s on septic, I’d want **system age and last pump date** in the proof column. Saves a lot of guessing.

The walk-away triggers are clean. Especially the part about legal use, insurability, and utility service moving the price. That’s the whole game.

If you want to keep going, the next useful step is a **fill-in worksheet by address** — one page, with a pass / renegotiate / walk decision at the bottom. That’d be the version I’d actually use.
